label: Threat Model
description: STRIDE-style threat model for a system or feature

fields.label: What it does & key components
fields.placeholder: Handles card payments via Stripe; API, worker, Postgres, S3 receipts.

fields.label: Trust boundaries & entry points
fields.placeholder: Public API gateway, internal service mesh, third-party webhook, admin panel.

fields.label: Known assumptions / out of scope
fields.placeholder: Cloud provider assumed trusted; physical security out of scope.

label: Code Security Audit
description: Deep secure-code review of a codebase or component

fields.label: What the code does & where code goes
fields.placeholder: JWT auth + session service. Paste code or describe modules below.

template:
You are a principal security engineer performing an adversarial secure-code review of "{{targetName}}" ({{language}}).

## Context
{{context}}

- Reference standard: {{standard}}
- Priority focus: {{focus}}

## Review method
- Read for intent, then think like an attacker: trace untrusted input to every sink.
- For each finding give: location, vulnerability class (with CWE id), exploit scenario, and {{#if severity}}a CVSS-style severity with vector{{else}}a severity rating{{/if}}.
- Distinguish confirmed issues from suspected ones; avoid false positives and explain reasoning.
{{#if fixes}}- Provide a minimal, correct patched code snippet for each high/critical finding.
{{/if}}- Note any systemic patterns (missing input validation layer, weak crypto defaults) rather than only instances.

## Deliverables
1. Executive summary with risk posture in 3-4 sentences.
2. Findings table ordered by severity, mapped to {{standard}}.
3. Detailed write-ups{{#if fixes}} with patches{{/if}} and a prioritized fix order.

If code is not fully provided, audit what is given and clearly flag the assumptions made for the rest.

label: Penetration Test Plan

fields.label: In-scope targets & description
fields.placeholder: app.example.com, api.example.com, mobile app v4. Exclude prod DB and 3rd-party SaaS.

fields.label: Rules of engagement & windows
fields.placeholder: Test window 02:00-06:00 UTC; no DoS; rate-limit aware; emergency contact on call.

template:
You are a lead penetration tester scoping the engagement "{{engagementName}}" following the {{methodology}} methodology.

## Scope
{{scope}}

- Test types: {{testType}}
- Knowledge level: {{knowledge}} testing

## Plan requirements
- Define clear objectives, in/out-of-scope assets, and authorization prerequisites.
- Lay out phases (recon, scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, reporting) with tooling and techniques per phase mapped to {{methodology}}.
- Specify rules of engagement: test windows, prohibited actions, data-handling, and escalation/abort criteria.
{{#if constraints}}- Honor these constraints: {{constraints}}
{{/if}}- Define severity rubric and how findings will be reported and tracked.
{{#if retest}}- Include a remediation retest phase with pass/fail criteria.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. Engagement charter (objectives, scope, RoE, contacts).
2. Phase-by-phase test plan with techniques and tooling.
3. Reporting template and severity rubric{{#if retest}} plus retest checklist{{/if}}.

Assume a standard professional engagement and fill gaps with industry-standard defaults; flag only authorization-blocking ambiguities.

label: Security Policy

fields.label: Organization context
fields.placeholder: 120-person SaaS company, remote-first, AWS, handles customer PII.

template:
You are a senior GRC and information-security policy author. Draft a "{{policyName}}" ({{policyType}} policy) that is clear, enforceable, and audit-ready.

## Organization context
{{orgContext}}

- Primary audience: {{audience}}
{{#if frameworks}}- Align controls to: {{frameworks}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}

## Requirements
- Use plain, unambiguous language with defined terms; avoid vague "should" where "must" is intended.
- Standard structure: purpose, scope, definitions, policy statements, roles & responsibilities{{#if enforcement}}, enforcement, and exceptions/waivers{{/if}}, review cadence.
- Make each requirement testable and map key statements to the named frameworks' controls.
- Be realistic for the org size described; no aspirational controls that cannot be operated.

## Deliverables
1. The complete policy document, ready to circulate.
2. A control-mapping table (policy statement -> framework control id).
3. A short rollout note: who approves, who is trained, and the review interval.

Fill organizational gaps with sensible industry defaults and label any such assumptions inline.

label: Incident Response Playbook
description: Step-by-step IR playbook for a specific incident type

fields.label: Environment & tooling
fields.placeholder: AWS + GitHub, EDR on endpoints, SIEM = Splunk, IdP = Okta, 24x7 on-call.

template:
You are an incident-response lead writing an operational playbook: "{{playbookName}}" for a {{incidentType}} incident, following the {{framework}} lifecycle.

## Environment
{{envContext}}
{{#if regulated}}
- Notification obligations to plan for: {{regulated}}
{{/if}}
## Playbook requirements
- Organize by lifecycle phase: preparation, detection & analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, post-incident.
- For each phase give concrete, ordered actions with the responsible role, expected evidence to collect, and decision points.
- Be specific to the tooling described (exact consoles/commands where reasonable), not generic advice.
{{#if regulated}}- Include a notification-timeline checklist for the listed obligations with trigger criteria.
{{/if}}{{#if raci}}- Include a RACI matrix and ready-to-send internal/external/legal communication templates.
{{/if}}{{#if tabletop}}- Add a realistic tabletop exercise scenario with injects and discussion questions.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The phase-by-phase runbook with roles and evidence handling.
2. Severity classification and escalation criteria.
3. A post-incident review template{{#if tabletop}} and the tabletop scenario{{/if}}.

Use battle-tested defaults for any unspecified detail and call out where local validation is required.

label: Vulnerability Triage
description: Prioritize and route a batch of reported vulnerabilities

fields.label: Findings to triage
fields.placeholder: Paste CVEs/scanner output: CVE-2024-XXXX in lib X (transitive), reflected XSS on /search, ...

fields.label: Asset context & exposure
fields.placeholder: Internet-facing API handling PII; lib X used only in offline batch job, not the API path.

template:
You are a security engineer running vulnerability triage for the batch "{{batchName}}" using a {{scoring}} prioritization model.

## Findings
{{findings}}

## Asset context
{{assetContext}}

## Triage method
- For each finding determine: real exploitability in THIS context, exposure, reachability of the vulnerable code path, and business impact.
- Down-rank non-reachable / non-exploitable items and explain why; up-rank actively exploited or internet-exposed ones.
- Assign a final priority and recommended action (patch, mitigate, accept-with-justification, monitor).
{{#if slas}}- Map each priority to these SLAs: {{slas}}
{{/if}}{{#if owners}}- Suggest a likely owning team and a concise ticket title + description per actionable item.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. A ranked triage table (finding, context-adjusted severity, priority, action, rationale).
2. The "fix now" shortlist with justification.
3. Items recommended for risk acceptance with the reasoning to record.

Where context is thin, state the assumption and triage conservatively rather than guessing optimistically.

label: Secure Design Review
description: Security review of an architecture or design doc

fields.label: Design summary / doc
fields.placeholder: Users mint scoped API keys in dashboard; keys stored hashed; rate-limited; webhook on rotation.

template:
You are a security architect conducting a {{stage}} secure design review of "{{featureName}}" for an audience of {{audience}}.

## Proposed design
{{designDoc}}

- Domains to weight heavily: {{concerns}}

## Review approach
- Restate the security-relevant assumptions and trust boundaries you infer from the design.
- Evaluate the design against secure-by-design principles: least privilege, fail-safe defaults, defense in depth, complete mediation, and minimized attack surface.
- For each concern, identify weaknesses, give a concrete attack scenario, and recommend a specific design change (not just "add validation").
- Separate must-fix-before-build issues from improvements and nice-to-haves.
{{#if checklist}}- Produce a security acceptance checklist the team must satisfy before implementation.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. Inferred trust model and assumptions.
2. Findings grouped as blocking / recommended / optional, each with a remediation.
3. A clear go / go-with-changes / no-go recommendation{{#if checklist}} plus the acceptance checklist{{/if}}.

Make well-reasoned assumptions where the doc is silent and mark them explicitly so the author can confirm.
